# Design Doc: Compactor for DataObjects in Loki

# Background

To accommodate its increasing scaling requirements, Loki is adopting a new data encoding and storage format. This new format encodes logs and associated metadata in a [DataObject](https://github.com/grafana/loki/blob/c8daf493f6b0d765e3bcd52e19377b8c2bf116a3/pkg/dataobj/README.md).

Here is how the ingestion path building DataObjects looks:  
![][image1]

Introducing the complexities of buffering and other processes into our DataObject build pipeline, to create DataObjects with query-optimised properties/dimensions, is something we aim to avoid. Since we have a single Kafka topic for all tenants, a single partition could end up containing data from tenants with variable ingestion volumes, which in turn becomes the source for building the multi-tenant DataObjects.

Although we have designed segmentation keys and use shuffle-sharding to co-locate data from a single tenant across a limited number of Kafka partitions, it does not prevent multiple tenants from ending up on the same Kafka partition.

# Problem

With the current DataObjects build pipeline, we end up having the following problems:

* **Querying inefficiencies with multi-tenant DataObjects:** Since DataObject consumers must read data sequentially from the Kafka partitions, which contain data from different tenants, large tenants will occupy the majority of the space in the DataObject. This causes querying to be inefficient, especially for small to medium-sized tenants, as their data may be spread too thinly across multiple objects.  
* **Retention/Deletion inefficiencies with multi-tenant DataObjects:** Since we mix data from different tenants, we end up mixing data from tenants with varying retention periods. To selectively remove data from a multi-tenant DataObject would require us to rewrite the objects, which not only makes enforcing retention inefficient but also might cause DataObjects to be smaller than the expected size for tenants with higher retention requirements. Similar problems arise when removing data during the processing of delete requests.  
* **Incorrect query results occur due to duplicate data caused by retries:** Duplicate log lines within the same DataObjects are deduplicated while building them. However, duplicate log lines could end up in different DataObjects based on various factors, and would not be deduplicated at ingestion time. To correctly identify and eliminate duplicate log entries at query time, we must examine the entire log lines and their timestamps across all the DataObjects being touched by a query. Since we lack an efficient method to identify duplicate log lines across DataObjects at query time, we often serve incorrect query results when duplicate log entries are present.

# Goals

Design an L1 compaction strategy to rewrite DataObjects with the following properties:

* Per-tenant DataObjects.  
* Dedupe data while merging uncompacted objects.  
* Pave the way for \> L1 DataObject compaction.

# Non-Goals

* Design \>L1 compaction of DataObjects.  
* Add support for log deletion and enforcement of retention with DataObjects.

# Proposals

## Proposal 0: Do nothing

We will continue to rely on whatever improvements the DataObject build pipeline on the ingestion path can accommodate to shape/size the objects it creates.

Pros:

* No added complexity.

Cons:

* We will continue to serve incorrect results due to duplicate data unless we find a way to deduplicate logs at query time.  
* Queries require touching too many objects for small and medium tenants due to their data being spread too thin.  
* Removing logs that are out of retention or requested for deletion would be a resource-intensive operation and may result in objects being smaller than expected.

## Proposal 1: Use planner-worker style for compaction

For this proposal, we will leverage the existing horizontally scalable compactor architecture, which has been running in production for over a month, for processing delete requests with line filters. The implementation is extensible to add support for new kinds of jobs by implementing the required interfaces.

In this proposal, the DataObjects compaction work would be broken down into the following phases:  
![][image2]

### Planning phase

In this phase, the main compactor would discover a batch of uncompacted DataObjects to be compacted. After finding all the objects to be considered in the current iteration, the main compactor would analyse the source objects to determine how to merge data for each tenant to build one or more tenant-specific compacted objects (more on this below). The outcome of this phase would be a detailed plan, which would specify what data to be extracted from a set of source objects to build a new, compacted object.

The compaction plan would be preserved in the object storage, enabling the compactor to complete the execution of an existing plan after a rollout/restart, which would otherwise result in dangling objects built due to the partial execution of a compaction plan.

The implementation for executing the compaction plan must be idempotent, so that when part of or the entire plan is re-executed, we end up building exactly the same artefacts (including names, where relevant) as the previous attempts.

![][image3]

#### Target dimensions for Compacted DataObjects

Depending on the number of uncompacted DataObjects in storage, we may be dealing with either too much or too little data to compact. To make the object sizes consistent as much as possible, we will target compacted objects to be limited by the following dimensions(configurable):

* Uncompressed size  
* Max Timerange

#### Uncompacted DataObjects Discovery

*For the sake of simplicity, let's assume 1GB for the target uncompressed size and 2 hours for the maximum timerange. To make it easier to relate to the information below, I will refer to the assumed target dimension values in the following text instead of the dimension names.*

Because the volume of uncompacted DataObjects varies between tenants, smaller tenants might not generate enough data to reach the target compressed size. To prevent premature data compaction, we introduce a time-based requirement.

Compaction would be triggered only when at least one uncompacted DataObject is older than 2 hours. This ensures that when compaction occurs, we have a minimum of 2 hours of logs available for processing.

We have the following options to consider for how we pick the log compaction window:

1. **Floating window:** With this option, the window starts from the oldest log of the oldest uncompacted object and ends at the 2-hour mark from the window's start. (👍 1\)

   Pros:  
   * Simpler to implement

   Cons:

   * Indeterministic object ranges  
       
2. **UTC-aligned rolling window:** With this option, the window rolls in 2-hour intervals using UTC. For example, each day would have the following compaction windows: 0-2h, 2-4h, 4-6h UTC. (👍 0\)

	Pros:

* Deterministic object ranges(at least for low-volume tenants)  
  * It may provide us with a more effective higher-level compaction strategy.

#### Compaction plan

One of the goals of compaction is to deduplicate log entries across different DataObjects. Considering that log entries are considered duplicates only if they belong to the same stream, we must merge the same streams from different DataObjects together in the new compacted object.

At a high level, we will maintain the same segmentation strategy as DataObject consumers to co-locate data from the same service together. Within each segment, we will consider merging logs from the same streams in a single DataObject. The compaction plan would list out what streams to pick from which objects to build a new compacted object.

To strictly adhere to the 2-hour compaction window, it will be necessary to split any multi-tenant DataObjects that contain data extending past this window. The truncated DataObjects must then be uploaded to ObjectStorage to ensure the safety of marking all original source objects for deletion. An alternative option would be to add DataObject-level markers to eliminate part of its data while querying and future compaction.

### Compaction phase

In this phase, the main compactor would build DataObject compaction jobs based on the compaction plan created in the previous phase. It would make a compaction job for each compacted DataObject it expects to produce, as per the compaction plan. The main compactor would add jobs to the job queue, which would be picked up by the compactor workers for concurrent execution.

After the successful execution of the compaction job, workers would respond with the details of the compacted DataObject that they uploaded to the object storage, which would then be accumulated by the compactor for the Indexing phase.

We will store the entire DataObjects structure (including compacted DataObjects, IndexObjects, and ToCs) under the tenant prefix after compaction, which achieves two main benefits. Firstly, all a tenant's data remains under a single prefix. Secondly, this approach prevents the uncontrolled expansion of the multi-tenant Tables of Contents (ToCs).

### Indexing phase

The compactor's primary role in this phase is to create new IndexObjects and update the Table of Contents (ToC) files to reference the newly compacted objects. During the Indexing phase, we will only update the Indexes to manage the discoverability of objects, allowing us to safely remove the original objects later without impacting already running queries while the updates are being applied.

A key challenge is that compaction temporarily results in duplicate data, as both the original and compacted DataObjects exist until the source objects are cleaned up. Since efficiently deduplicating logs within DataObjects is not feasible, the index update must be atomic. This atomicity is crucial to ensure that ongoing queries continue to access the original, uncompacted objects, while all new queries immediately begin using the recently created, compacted objects.

Since object storage lacks support for atomic updates across multiple objects, we cannot update all ToC files atomically when a single IndexObject is referenced by several of them. Considering all the constraints, instead of directly updating ToCs, we will batch updates in a common ToC updates file, which the Main Compactor will manage.

To signify upcoming queries that should ignore the soon-to-be-removed source objects, we will use Tombstones, which will also serve as Deletion Markers for the IndexObjects and DataObjects. The compactor would run a separate background process to apply the batched ToC updates and remove tombstoned objects. More on it later.

Whenever an attempt is made to discover IndexObjects, the batched updates would be factored in to eliminate any Tombstoned objects and include the newly created Objects.

The Indexing phase would be broken down into two sequential operations:

1. **Indexing Objects:** Since building IndexObjects is not a lightweight operation, we will utilise the Worker pool by adding support for the Index building job to the workers. In the Indexing Job, we will specify a batch of DataObjects to index. The worker would respond with the details of the new IndexObject it built and stored in the Object Storage.

2. **Update ToC file:** We will update the common ToC updates file to add a Tombstone for all the IndexObjects whose DataObjects were compacted away. Additionally, we will add Index entries for the new IndexObjects we built in the previous operation. All the updates would be applied atomically to the common ToC updates file.

### Cleanup loop

The cleanup loop would run independently in the Main Compactor to consistently perform the following operations:

* Tombstoned objects will be permanently deleted, along with their corresponding entries in the ToC updates file, 30 minutes after the tombstone is generated. The original entries from the actual ToC files would also be removed.  
* Index entries for the new IndexObjects would be moved from the common ToC updates file to the relevant time-sharded ToC files.

# Consensus

*Document the discussion and opinion here, and which of the above options is the agreed upon approach once they have been discussed \- discussion can occur within the document through comments, or by having a meeting where the document is read in advance and the proposals debated. Be sure to document who this consensus represents.*
